mnemonics: If more pigeons than holes, one hole must have at least two pigeons.
The Overflow Principle: When you put more items into containers than there are containers, at least one container must hold more than one item.
keyPoints: The Pigeonhole Principle states that if n items are put into m containers, with n > m, then at least one container must contain more than one item.
It is a powerful tool for proving the existence of a property without needing to find the specific instance.
The principle can be generalized: if n items are put into m containers, then at least one container contains at least ceil(n/m) items.
commonMistakes: Assuming the principle only applies to exact numbers; it applies to any case where the number of items exceeds the number of categories.
Forgetting that the principle only proves existence, not quantity or location. It doesn't tell you which pigeonhole has the extra pigeons or how many extra there are.
